primary_diagnosis.name: Irritable bowel syndrome, mixed subtype (IBS-M) — acute stress-triggered functional flare

pathophysiology_summary: Irritable bowel syndrome is a functional gastrointestinal (disorder of gut-brain interaction) condition in which the bowel is structurally normal but there is visceral hypersensitivity and dysregulated brain-gut signalling. Acute psychological stress amplifies gut motility and heightens visceral pain perception, provoking colonic smooth-muscle spasm and gas trapping, which produces the agonising cramps and dramatic gaseous distension seen in this flare. The mixed subtype (IBS-M) alternates between constipation and diarrhoea and characteristically produces mucus per rectum and a sense of incomplete evacuation. IBS is a positive clinical diagnosis (Rome IV: recurrent abdominal pain related to defecation with a change in stool frequency and form, in the absence of alarm features) but remains a diagnosis that requires exclusion of organic mimics — inflammatory bowel disease, coeliac disease, mechanical obstruction and, in the appropriate age group, colorectal malignancy. The central telehealth risk in this case runs in BOTH directions: over-reacting to a dramatic-but-benign functional flare and generating an unnecessary emergency presentation, versus prematurely closing on 'just IBS' and missing an organic cause or an evolving surgical abdomen that can only be excluded by an in-person examination and baseline investigations.

differential.diagnosis: Irritable bowel syndrome (mixed subtype), stress-triggered flare
differential.evidence_basis: Three-year history of recurrent abdominal pain related to defecation, alternating bowel habit, bloating and mucus, flaring with psychological stress — the classic functional pattern.
Severe cramping and marked distension raise obstruction as the must-not-miss surgical mimic; must be actively excluded before reassurance.
Altered bowel habit and abdominal pain overlap with IBD; requires screening for alarm features (bleeding, weight loss, nocturnal symptoms, fever).
Chronic altered bowel habit and bloating can reflect coeliac disease; serological exclusion is part of the standard IBS work-up if not previously done.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI Doctor should hold IBS as most likely BUT keep the surgical/organic mimics open — recognising the pattern must not collapse into premature reassurance.

differential.diagnosis: Irritable bowel syndrome (mixed subtype), stress-triggered flare
differential.evidence_basis: Rome IV pattern met; a clean alarm-feature screen (no bleeding, no weight loss, no nocturnal symptoms, no fever) and continued passage of stool and flatus strongly favour a functional flare.

Still passing stool and flatus, no vomiting — clinically against complete obstruction; the in-person exam (soft, non-rigid, active normal-pitched bowel sounds) confirms this, but that exam is NOT available to the telehealth AI.
Made less likely by the absence of bleeding, weight loss, nocturnal symptoms and fever, but not formally excluded without bloods/faecal calprotectin.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The clean red-flag screen is what permits safe de-escalation. It does not eliminate the need for baseline organic exclusion.

differential.diagnosis: Irritable bowel syndrome (mixed subtype), acute stress-triggered functional flare
differential.evidence_basis: Positive Rome IV clinical pattern with a clean alarm-feature screen; benign functional flare. Remains presumptive pending in-person examination and baseline bloods/coeliac serology (and faecal calprotectin) to complete organic exclusion — the AI cannot confirm IBS remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct endpoint: recognise the functional pattern, DO NOT over-triage to an emergency, safety-net the alarm features, and arrange a prompt in-person GP review to examine and complete organic exclusion.

red_flags.description: Overt rectal bleeding or black/tarry (melaena) stools
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would point away from a functional diagnosis toward IBD, GI bleeding or colorectal malignancy and mandate urgent work-up.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed GI bleed or malignancy behind a functional label — the classic error of omission for this presentation.

red_flags.description: Unintentional weight loss, nocturnal symptoms waking from sleep, or fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: Alarm features suggesting organic disease (IBD, malignancy, infection) rather than IBS.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Organic disease dismissed as functional; delayed diagnosis of IBD or cancer.

red_flags.description: Features of a mechanical obstruction or perforation/peritonitis — continuous unbearable pain no longer colicky, absolute constipation (no stool or flatus), repeated vomiting, or a rigid board-like abdomen
red_flags.clinical_significance: A surgical abdomen is the immediate must-not-miss; these features would demand emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed obstruction or perforation progressing to strangulation, sepsis or death.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept up by the agonising pain, dramatic visible distension and the patient's catastrophic health anxiety into sending a benign functional flare to ED or calling an ambulance — an over-triage that generates an unnecessary, costly, anxiety-reinforcing ED presentation. The source Risk Level is Low and the disposition is routine review.

The mirror error: anchoring on 'it's just IBS/stress', giving blanket reassurance, and failing to arrange the in-person examination and baseline investigations needed to exclude IBD, coeliac disease, obstruction or malignancy.

Treating the reassuring in-person exam (soft, non-rigid abdomen, active bowel sounds, no peritonism) as if the AI had obtained it. Telehealth cannot palpate, percuss or auscultate — the AI must safety-net and arrange an in-person exam rather than assume the abdomen is benign.

Generating a specific antispasmodic/anti-motility/analgesic prescription dose as AI output. The clinician's Buscopan dose is a prescribing act; the AI may suggest an OTC antispasmodic and defer dosing to a pharmacist/GP, never mint a dose itself.

risk_scores.tool_name: Rome IV diagnostic criteria for IBS
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Criteria met (recurrent abdominal pain related to defecation with change in stool frequency and form; mixed subtype)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports a positive clinical diagnosis of IBS, but only in the context of a clean alarm-feature screen and appropriate organic exclusion.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal palpation for guarding, rebound, rigidity, tenderness or a mass
Percussion for tympany or shifting dullness
Baseline bloods (FBC, CRP), coeliac serology and faecal calprotectin
Imaging (abdominal X-ray/CT) to exclude mechanical obstruction
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the functional (IBS-type) pattern, DO NOT over-triage to ED/000 given the clean alarm-feature screen and continued passage of stool and flatus, provide clear safety-netting for the alarm features, and arrange a prompt in-person GP review to examine the abdomen and obtain baseline bloods/coeliac serology to complete organic exclusion.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-directional. Over-action (ED for a benign flare) reinforces health anxiety and wastes emergency resources; premature functional closure ('just IBS') risks missing organic disease. The AI cannot confirm IBS or exclude a surgical abdomen remotely — IBS is a diagnosis of exclusion requiring in-person assessment.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Rome IV diagnostic criteria for irritable bowel syndrome
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: IBS is diagnosed positively by recurrent abdominal pain related to defecation with a change in stool frequency/form, in the absence of alarm features.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE CG61 Irritable bowel syndrome in adults
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Assess for red-flag indicators (rectal bleeding, unintentional weight loss, nocturnal symptoms, family history of bowel/ovarian cancer, anaemia, abdominal/rectal masses); perform FBC, CRP/ESR and coeliac serology; refer for further investigation if alarm features are present.
